summary: The q3 dataset contains 10 problems (q1-q10), each registering exactly 100 issues, indicating uniform activity across the board with no single standout in traction. The proof status section provides only issue counts without resolution states, so solvability and progress cannot be reliably inferred from the available data. Additional metadata on issue type, severity, and proof completion is required to produce meaningful prioritization.
problems: All ten problems (q1-q10) report an identical count of 100 issues, suggesting either a placeholder/default value or a data export artifact rather than genuine differential activity.
No proof status states (e.g., open, resolved, in-progress) are provided, only raw issue counts, preventing assessment of which problems are near completion.
No metadata on issue category, severity, or recency exists, making it impossible to identify common patterns or root causes in the open issues.
Lack of per-problem differentiation means no problem can currently be flagged as having more traction or being closer to solvable than another.
highlights: Complete coverage: all ten problems have logged issues, indicating active engagement across the entire dataset.
Consistent data structure across problems should make automated reporting straightforward once richer metadata is added.
Uniform 100-issue counts make this an ideal candidate for establishing a baseline and instrumenting better tracking going forward.

suggested_todos.title: Audit the data pipeline to confirm whether the uniform 100-issue counts are real or a default/placeholder value
Augment issue records with status fields (open/in-progress/resolved) to enable solvability analysis
Add issue categorization and severity tagging to surface common patterns across q1-q10
Define metrics for 'traction' and 'near-solvable' so future reports can rank problems meaningfully
Schedule a follow-up report once enriched metadata is available to identify next-week priorities
